region_of_origin: Guangdong, China; widely served in Singapore hawker centres and siu mei shops
food_subgroup: Cantonese roasted pork belly with crispy crackling skin, subcutaneous fat layer, and lean meat (siu mei tradition)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Siu Yuk as a prepared Cantonese hawker dish. Closest parent would be roasted pork belly; no exact FoodOn term available as of 2026.
ontology.foodex2_description: Roasted pork belly with crispy skin (closest parent match — FoodEx2 A0KCA)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0KCA is the closest defensible parent for roasted pork belly. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for Siu Yuk as a Singapore hawker mixed-layer preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code found for Siu Yuk Cantonese roast pork belly as a prepared dish.

serving.description: 100g cooked mixed serving (~30g pork skin/crackling + ~25g subcutaneous fat layer + ~45g lean meat) — Singapore hawker economy rice standard portion
serving.natural_language_portion: A standard Singapore hawker economy rice serving of Siu Yuk is approximately 100g of mixed pork belly, comprising roughly 30g of crispy crackling skin, 25g of subcutaneous fat, and 45g of lean meat, typically served sliced over steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: Siu Yuk — HPB Singapore Foodo + Component Calculation (pork belly layers); cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central pork belly entries
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived from HPB Singapore Foodo database for Siu Yuk and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central pork belly (roasted) entries. The mixed-layer composition (30g skin + 25g subcutaneous fat + 45g lean) is a Singapore hawker standard portion estimate. Values represent a weighted composite of the three anatomical layers. Energy 450 kcal, protein 14g, fat 39g, carbohydrate 2g per 100g are consistent with HPB and USDA data for roasted pork belly with skin.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index estimation for roasted pork belly — analogous to high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat preparations; GI ~20 based on predominantly fat and protein macronutrient profile with minimal available carbohydrate (~2g per 100g from marinade residue)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (analogous estimate basis); peer-reviewed literature on GI of meat and high-fat preparations
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Siu Yuk in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~20 is estimated by analogy with other high-fat, low-carbohydrate roasted pork preparations. Available carbohydrate is approximately 2g per 100g (from marinade residue), yielding a glycemic load of ~0.4 per 100g serving. This is consistent with the negligible glycaemic impact expected from a predominantly fat-and-protein food. The GI value of ~20 is a conservative upper-bound estimate; the true GI may be lower or not meaningfully measurable.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~700mg per 100g) from salt-curing process
hypertension.guidance: Siu Yuk contains approximately 700mg sodium per 100g — a typical economy rice serving of 80–120g delivers 560–840mg sodium from the meat alone, representing 24–36% of the 2300mg daily sodium limit; hypertensive patients should limit portion size to 60g or fewer and request lean-only cuts without skin to reduce both sodium and saturated fat load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high caloric density (450 kcal/100g) and fat load (39g/100g) despite negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~20, GL ~0.4)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Siu Yuk has negligible glycaemic impact (GI ~20, GL ~0.4 per 100g) and does not raise blood glucose acutely; however, the very high caloric density (450 kcal/100g) and fat content (39g/100g) contribute to overall energy excess and insulin resistance risk in Type 2 Diabetes patients, so portion size should be limited to 60–80g per meal and skin/fat layers avoided where possible.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high saturated fat (14.5g/100g), high total fat (39g/100g), high sodium (700mg/100g), and cholesterol (90mg/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Siu Yuk is among the highest-saturated-fat hawker meats in Singapore (14.5g saturated fat per 100g, predominantly palmitic and stearic acids from the crackling skin and subcutaneous fat layer); CVD patients should avoid the skin and subcutaneous fat entirely, limit lean-meat portions to 60g per serving, and substitute with lower-fat proteins such as soy sauce chicken, steamed fish, or tofu on most days.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High saturated fat (14.5g/100g) associated with pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid pathway activation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For rheumatoid arthritis patients, Siu Yuk should be consumed in strict moderation (maximum 60g per serving, without skin); request 'no skin' (无皮) and 'lean only' (瘦肉) to reduce saturated fat intake, and replace with anti-inflammatory protein sources such as fish, tofu, or soy sauce chicken on most days.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Siu Yuk (烧肉 / 燒肉), also known as Siu Bak, is a classic Cantonese roasted pork belly dish that is a centrepiece of the siu mei (烧味) roasted meats tradition originating in Guangdong, China. In Singapore, it is ubiquitous at economy rice (cai png) stalls, dedicated siu mei shops, and Chinese restaurants. The dish is prepared by salt-curing a whole pork belly slab with five-spice powder, garlic, Shaoxing wine, and sugar, then roasting at high heat to achieve the signature blistered, crackling skin (pork rind). The result is a three-layer structure: crispy crackling skin on top, a thick layer of soft subcutaneous fat beneath, and lean pork meat at the base. A standard Singapore hawker mixed serving of 100g comprises approximately 30g crackling skin, 25g subcutaneous fat, and 45g lean meat. Nutritionally, Siu Yuk is the highest-fat hawker meat in Singapore: 450 kcal, 14g protein, 39g total fat (14.5g saturated), 2g carbohydrate, and 700mg sodium per 100g. The crackling skin alone contains approximately 35g fat per 100g skin, and the subcutaneous fat layer is approximately 90% fat. Glycaemic impact is negligible (GI ~20, GL ~0.4) due to the minimal carbohydrate content. For patients with cardiovascular disease, rheumatoid arthritis, or hypertension, the skin and fat layers should be avoided; requesting lean-only (瘦肉, 无皮) reduces fat by approximately 23g and calories by approximately 185 kcal per 100g. Siu Yuk is gluten-free and dairy-free. Key nutrients include selenium (22 mcg), niacin (4mg), vitamin B6 (0.4mg), zinc (2mg), and phosphorus (150mg).
common_user_queries: siu yuk for high blood pressure

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values (energy 450 kcal, protein 14g, fat 39g, carbohydrate 2g, sodium 700mg per 100g) are consistent with HPB Singapore Foodo database entries for Siu Yuk and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central roasted pork belly data. The mixed-layer composition is a well-documented Singapore hawker standard. Confidence is high for macros and secondary nutrients; GI is an analogous estimate (confidence 0.9) as no direct GI measurement exists for Siu Yuk.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Siu Yuk as a Singapore hawker mixed-layer preparation (skin + subcutaneous fat + lean) is not separately reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the composite mixed-layer preparation is not reported in the primary sources consulted. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae and vitamin_c_mg are 0.0: confirmed negligible/zero per HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central for roasted pork belly; these are naturally zero or trace for this food type. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a pure meat product with no plant-fibre components. 3. GI ~20 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Siu Yuk exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The estimate is based on the negligible available carbohydrate (~2g/100g from marinade residue) and the predominantly fat-and-protein macronutrient profile. 4. FoodEx2 code A0KCA is used as the closest defensible parent (roasted pork belly with crispy skin); no exact FoodEx2 code exists for Siu Yuk as a Singapore hawker composite preparation. FoodOn URI is null: no exact FoodOn term available for this prepared hawker dish. 5. Halal flag is false: Siu Yuk is pork-based and not halal. 6. The input record used 'diabetes' as a condition key; this has been corrected to 'type2_diabetes' with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' per Veritas scoring rules. 7. Hypertension suitability from input was marked 'suitable: true' with a moderate-high sodium note; this has been reclassified as concern_level 'high' in the health_context to accurately reflect the 700mg/100g sodium load relative to clinical guidelines. 8. Q8 fix (iteration 3): aliases_by_language tamil entries ('சியு யுக் — கண்டோனீஸ் வறுத்த பன்றி வயிறு' and 'சியு யுக்') and hindi entries ('सियु युक — कैंटोनीज़ भुना हुआ सूअर का पेट' and 'सியु युक') were added verbatim to the flat aliases array to ensure full consistency between aliases_by_language and the flat aliases list. Self-check performed: every string value in every aliases_by_language language key appears verbatim in the flat aliases array.

Corrected health_context condition key from 'diabetes' to 'type2_diabetes'. Added cardiovascular_disease health_context entry. Expanded aliases_by_language. Documented -1 and 0.0 sentinel values in notes. Expanded natural_language_description. Added gi_source with University of Sydney GI Database. Added foodex2_gap_note, snomed_ct_gap_note fields.
Iteration 2: Attempted q8 alias consistency fix — added Malay, Tamil, Hindi aliases to flat array. Partial fix only; Tamil and Hindi full-form strings were not fully mirrored.

Iteration 3 (current): Full q8 remediation — performed exhaustive self-check of every string value in every aliases_by_language language key against flat aliases array. Added missing verbatim entries: 'சியு யுக் — கண்டோனீஸ் வறுத்த பன்றி வயிறு', 'சியு யுக்', 'सियु युक — कैंटोनीज़ भुना हुआ सूअर का पेट', 'सियु युक' to flat aliases. Removed 'Siu Yuk (zh-hant)' from aliases_by_language chinese (was not a valid Chinese-language alias) and replaced with the three correct Chinese entries.
